sanskrit_devanagari:
यस्त्वात्मरतिरेव स्यादात्मतृप्तश्च मानवः ।
आत्मन्येव च सन्तुष्टस्तस्य कार्यं न विद्यते ॥ १७ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
yas tv ātma-ratir eva syād
ātma-tṛptaś ca mānavaḥ |
ātmany eva ca santuṣṭas
tasya kāryaṁ na vidyate || 17 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: But for one who takes pleasure in the Self, whose human life is one of self-realization, and who is satisfied in the Self only, fully satiated – for him there is no duty.
(See holy-bhagavad-gita.org for full Mukundananda rendering; agreement with Prabhupada noted in disagreements section where it diverges materially.)

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: A person who is fully Kṛṣṇa conscious, and is fully satisfied by his acts in Kṛṣṇa consciousness, no longer has any duty to perform. Due to his being Kṛṣṇa conscious, all impiety within is instantly cleansed, an effect of many, many thousands of yajña performances.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.17 Tu, but; that manavah, man, the sannyasin, the man of Knowledge, steadfast in the knowledge of the Self; yah, who; atmaratih eva syat, rejoices only in the Self-not in the sense objects; and atma-trptah, who is satisfied only with the Self-not with food and drink; and is sant
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse as the formal exception to the karma-yoga injunction. The one who is established in Brahman-knowledge (ātma-rati = rejoicing in the Self, ātma-tṛpta = satisfied by the Self alone, ātmany eva santuṣṭa = content nowhere else) has no kāryaṁ (no duty-to-be-done) because karma operates in the field of avidyā and the realised one has crossed it. This is the Advaitic doctrinal anchor: jñāna-niṣṭhā exempts from karma.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.17 But for him, who is not in need of the means of Jnana Yoga and Karma Yoga, who finds delight in the self on his own, i.e., who is established in the self, who is satisfied by the self alone and not by food, drink and other things which are other than the self, who rejoices in
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the verse more narrowly: the one whose attention is on the Self (ātman as the inner self distinct from the body and gunas) has no remaining sense-object that compels action. But this does not mean the realised one ceases acting; it means the compulsion to act for sense-satisfaction is gone. The actor still continues to act for the welfare of the world (3.20), now as worship of the Lord rather than for self.

positions.rendering: self-realized... satisfied within the self... fully satiated by self-realization
rejoices in the self alone... content in the self... satisfied with the self
the renunciate established in knowledge of the Self (Brahman); for such a one no karma — Vedic-prescribed or otherwise — has any function
the one whose attention is fixed on the inner Self as distinct from prakṛti; this one no longer needs sense-object engagement, but still acts for the world

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The Advaita reading (Shankara) treats this verse as the doctrinal exemption-clause for the jñāna-niṣṭha — the renunciate established in knowledge has no karma. The Vishishtadvaita reading (Ramanuja) reads it more narrowly as a description of the realised actor whose interior orientation has shifted, but who continues to act (per 3.20). The translators flatten this distinction by rendering the three Sanskrit qualifiers (ātma-rati, ātma-tṛpta, ātmany eva santuṣṭa) as near-synonyms; the commentaries treat them as three doctrinal anchors specifying who exactly is exempted.

literal_meaning: But the man who rejoices in the Self alone, who is satisfied with the Self, content in the Self alone — for him there is no duty to be performed.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The verse names the exception to the rule of unavoidable action. For the realised one (the ātma-rati), the wheel of yajña no longer applies because such a one is not consuming from the wheel in the way that creates the obligation to contribute back. Krishna will immediately qualify this at 3.20-3.25: even the wise act for loka-saṅgraha (welfare of the world). The exception exists, but it is narrow, and the chapter is not directed at it.
